The renaissance of desktop applications has arrived, yet this transformation is marked by profound architectural trade-offs. The Electron framework, which empowered web developers to craft fully functional desktop solutions using JavaScript, HTML, and CSS, has become an industrial standard. However, for systems where reliability and security are paramount, such as ground control stations for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) or autonomous ground transport, choosing this platform evolves into a significant engineering dilemma.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Architectural Paradoxes in Modern Development
The concept behind Electron is rooted in embedding Chromium and Node.js into every application package. While this provides unparalleled speed in prototyping, the price of this convenience is reflected in excessive resource consumption. Unlike native software, which communicates directly with operating system APIs, Electron constructs a multi-layered architecture. Interface rendering occurs through a browser engine, creating an unavoidable distance between the code and the hardware. In real-time tasks, which are critical for drone flight control or autonomous vehicle navigation, any latency in rendering vital flight parameters can lead to catastrophic consequences.
The core issue lies in the fact that modern rendering requires significant computational power just to display a control panel. When an operator of an unmanned vehicle control system observes telemetry, they rely on the immediate responsiveness of the system. However, the Chromium engine, being a universal tool, is not optimized for specialized low-level monitoring tasks. This generates the “heavyweight” problem, where RAM is spent not on control logic, but on sustaining the browser environment within each process.
Security in the Era of Universal Engines
The fundamental ailment of Electron lies in its dependence on the browser ecosystem. Since every application is essentially an isolated instance of Chromium, any vulnerability in the core of the browser automatically becomes a critical flaw in your application’s security. In the context of ground control stations, which often operate within closed or secure perimeters, the need to constantly update the entire package to fix a single vulnerability in Chromium creates an immense risk.
Developers are forced to balance the necessity of deploying security patches with equipment stability. In the industrial sector, software updates often require rigorous, long-term testing. If your product depends on an external Electron update repository, you effectively lose control over your system’s security timeline. This creates an attack vector where a malicious actor can utilize exploits targeting widespread browser engines to gain access to infrastructure management.
Comparative Analysis of Software Development Approaches
Choosing a platform determines not only current costs but also the long-term sustainability of the system. The table below compares Electron with alternative approaches, such as native languages (C++, Rust) or frameworks with lower resource consumption (Tauri).
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Operational Specifics in Critical Industries
Applying Electron in the sphere of UAVs and ground transport imposes specific constraints on the product lifecycle. Our observations indicate that when scaling control systems to hundreds of simultaneous connections or complex data visualization scenarios, Electron begins to show signs of fatigue. The primary issue becomes performance degradation during long operating sessions, a common occurrence in monitoring centers.
Risk Management in Complex System Design
Risk migration is a key phase in quality management. When you build a traffic management system, you cannot afford sudden application crashes or interface freezes caused by memory leaks in the rendering script. To mitigate risks, we employ strict process isolation strategies; however, Electron itself hinders the creation of truly fault-tolerant control loops.

• Scheduled restarts of rendering processes to clear memory during operator shifts.

• Application of profiling tools to detect leaks in JavaScript code before they reach the end user.

• Use of custom logging systems that mirror critical telemetry data at a layer independent of the graphical interface.
The necessity of such compensatory measures indicates an inherent mismatch between the Electron architecture and the specifics of tasks requiring high reliability. When engineers must struggle against the platform itself simply to ensure the stability of basic functions, it becomes evident that the technology has hit its ceiling within your project.
Paths to Technological Independence
Considering alternatives, engineers often turn toward Rust, which ensures memory safety at the compilation level. Utilizing frameworks like Tauri allows keeping the frontend in familiar web technologies while replacing the heavy Chromium engine with native system WebViews. This drastically reduces the size of the application and eliminates the problem of directly inheriting every browser vulnerability.
However, transitioning to new tracks requires a deep overhaul of the existing codebase. For teams engaged in writing ground control stations, this process appears as a major investment project. It is crucial to understand that abandoning Electron is not merely switching a framework; it is changing the philosophy of interface design. Transitioning from “web-oriented” thinking to “system-oriented” thinking enables the creation of applications that perform faster, consume fewer resources, and, most importantly, are predictable under non-standard conditions in transport.
The Future of Ground Control in Industrial Design
The modern market demands flexibility from control systems, which web technologies provide, but with the reliability inherent to industrial controllers. Balancing between these two poles remains the primary challenge for engineers. In current realities, Electron remains a tool for rapid market entry, but for long-term UAV or autonomous transport control systems, a transition to architectures where the graphical interface is maximally separated from critical control logic is required.
Technological Debt and Long-Term Strategy
The technological debt accumulated while using Electron becomes evident after three to five years of active operation. As operating systems update and security requirements tighten, maintaining the application requires increasingly greater effort. For UAV control systems, it is critical to guarantee system behavior over decades.

• Separation of the interface layer and data processing logic into two independent processes.

• Transition to the use of low-level protocols for data exchange, excluding intermediaries like HTTP or WebSockets within the local network.

• Refusal to use external dependencies that cannot be audited for hidden backdoors or vulnerabilities.
Implementing these steps allows for the minimization of the negative influence of platform choice, even if you remain within the Electron ecosystem in the short term. True efficiency lies in understanding the boundaries of technological applicability and timely transition to more stable solutions where the stakes are high.
Decision-making in software architecture for ground control systems requires a sober assessment of risks. While Electron allows for the rapid assembly of a prototype that looks modern and works on any system, it brings invisible costs in maintenance, security risks, and performance limitations. For tasks where human lives and the safety of expensive equipment depend on software correctness, hybrid architectures must be considered.
The path for the development of control systems should lie through simplification. The fewer dependencies your application has, the easier it is to secure and maintain. In a world where UAVs and autonomous transport are becoming part of everyday infrastructure, code reliability ceases to be just a technical requirement and becomes the foundation of operational security. Investing in a transition to more compact and transparent platforms today will save thousands of development hours and prevent potential incidents in the future.
